narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 40% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic profile, including your Year 1 school decile of 2—well below the typical student's 5.71—your Year 3 decile of 2 compared to the average 5.53, your LSAT score of 32 versus the usual 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 against an average of 3.21. Your lower-middle family income bracket also factored in, along with your background as a Native American male planning full-time studies, which are less common in the applicant pool where most are White, about half female, and overwhelmingly full-time. When the model weighed these details, your Year 3 decile ranking stood out as the strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction, more so than any other factor. Right behind it was your LSAT score, which had the next biggest impact in that direction. Your Year 1 decile came third in influence, followed by family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender as the least impactful. Overall, these elements combined to make your profile riskier than typical students, whose stronger grades, higher test scores, and more common demographics often predict bar passage. None of your features pulled strongly in the positive direction here, highlighting areas where you fell short of averages that usually signal success. Looking ahead, you could realistically retake the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above 36.7, as it's one of the top influencers and changeable with preparation. Focusing on building a stronger academic record in any future coursework might help offset those low deciles over time. While family background and demographics aren't adjustable, demonstrating improvement in test scores and grades through targeted study or prep courses could shift future predictions in your favor. We encourage you to use this feedback as a roadmap—many students turn things around with focused effort. Keep pursuing your goals; persistence pays off.
